Motion For Reconsideration For Plaintiff?S Application For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment After Hearing
Off Calendar Mar-07-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF SIA CONSULTING CORPORATION's Motion For Reconsideration For Plaintiff'S Application For Issuance Of Writ Of Attachment After Hearing:

Plaintiff's motion for reconsideration of the Court's November 9, 2021 order granting in part Plaintiff's application for a right to attach order and writ of attachment is denied; however, the Court will issue a corrected order and direct the Clerk of the Court to issue a writ of attachment in accordance with the Court's original order. (See Felczer v. Apple Inc. (2021) 63 Cal.App.5th 406, 413 [the function of a nunc pro tunc order is merely to correct the record in order to bring the judgment into alignment with the court's intention at the time].) Plaintiff's motion is brought on the ground that the Clerk of the Court declined to execute a writ of attachment because the Court's order did not specify any particular property to be attached. Plaintiff's application, filed September 28, 2021, sought to attach "[a]ny property of a defendant who is not a natural person," but did not specify any particular property (such as bank accounts, real property, or other property). Plaintiff was not required to do so. Code of Civil Procedure section 484.020, subdivision (e) requires "[a] description of the property to be attached under the writ of attachment and a statement that the plaintiff is informed and believes that such property is subject to attachment." However, "[w]here the defendant is a corporation, a reference to 'all corporate property which is subject to attachment pursuant to subdivision (a) of Code of Civil Procedure Section 487.010' satisfies the requirements of this subdivision." (Id.; see also California Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2020) 9:908 ["(No need to identify the property to be attached."].) The Clerk therefore erred in declining to issue the writ. Plaintiff shall submit a revised proposed order that repeats the language of the original order and adds the language in question, followed by "including, but not limited to," a description of the property or properties sought to be attached.

Defendant's belated request, filed February 22, 2022, that the Court reconsider its order and consider new evidence is denied. Defendant did not file a timely motion for reconsideration. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1008(b) [motion must be filed within 10 days after service of written notice of entry of order].) Nor does Defendant show that the purported new evidence, the Hanna Declaration, was unavailable to it at the time of the original hearing. "Facts of which the party seeking reconsideration was aware at the time of the original ruling are not new or different." (In re Marriage of Herr (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 1463, 1468; see also New York Times Co. v. Superior Court (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 206, 212; Garcia v. Hejmadi (1997) 58 Cal.App.4th 674, 689-691.) Moreover, the moving party, via its affidavit, must demonstrate diligence and provide a satisfactory explanation for failing to offer the new evidence in the first instance. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1008; Herr, 174 Cal.App.4th at 1468.) Defendant fails to meet these requirements.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Application And Hear For Right To Attach Order And Order For Issuance

MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF SIA CONSULTING CORPORATION'S Notice Of Application And Hearing For Right To Attach Order And Order For Issuance:
Plaintiff SIA Consulting Corporation's application for a right to attach order and order for issuance of writ of attachment against Defendant TJ Development, Inc. is granted in the amount of $68,731.25, representing the principal unpaid contract amount of $43,584, plus $13,025.05 in interest through August 31, 2021 and attorneys' fees and costs incurred to date in the amount of $12,122.20. Plaintiff's showing satisfies each of the statutory factors for attachment. (Code Civ. Proc. secs. 483.010, 484.020.) Plaintiff's evidentiary objection to the Quan declaration (par. 3 & Ex. A) on grounds of lack of proper authentication, lack of personal knowledge, and hearsay is sustained. (See Generale Bank Nederland v. Eyes of the Beholder Ltd. (1998) 61 Cal.App.4th 1384, 1391-1392 [a proper foundation must be established for declarations and affidavits showing the declarant had personal knowledge of each fact stated].) The Court declines to exercise its discretion to include in the amount to be secured by the attachment an estimated amount for future costs and allowable attorney's fees. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 482.110(b).) The order is conditioned upon Plaintiff posting an undertaking in the amount of $10,000. (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 489.220.) Defendant's request for a continuance is denied.

Order is signed.

Defendant Tj Development Inc.'S Demurrer To The Complaint. (For Purposes Of Tentative Ruling Only, Part 2 Of 2)
Off Calendar Jul-06-2021 Off Calendar

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-06-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT TJ DEVELOPMENT, INC.'s DEMURRER to COMPLAINT:

Defendant TJ Development Inc.'s demurrer to the complaint overruled in its entirety.

Generally, "a demurrer is an attack against the complaint on its face" that should not be sustained unless the complaint shows that the action may not be pursued. (Yolo County Dept. of Social Services v. Municipal Court (1980) 107 Cal.App.3d 842, 846-7.) It acts as a mechanism that tests the sufficiency of a complaint as a matter of law. (Weimer v. Nationstar Mortgage, LLC (2020) 47 Cal.App.5th 341, 352.)

"In ruling on a demurrer, the trial court is required to construe the complaint liberally with a view of substantial justice between the parties." (See Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 452; C & H Foods Co. v. Hartford Ins. Co. (1984) 163 Cal.App.3d 1055, 1062.) The court gives the complaint "a reasonable interpretation, reading it as a whole and its parts in their context." (Weimer v. Nationstar Mortgage, LLC (2020) 47 Cal.App.5th at 352.) The complaint can survive a general demurrer so long as the plaintiff discloses facts of some right to relief. (Id.) Here, Defendant contends Plaintiff failed to sufficiently state facts to allege the breach of contract and common count claims. (Code Civ. Proc., Sec. 430.10, subds. (e) & (g).)

Under California law, "a cause of action for breach of contract requires proof of the following elements: (1) existence of the contract; (2) plaintiff's performance or excuse for nonperformance; (3) defendant's breach; and (4) damages to plaintiff as a result of the breach." (Miles v. Deutsche Bank National Trust Co. (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 394, 401-402 [complaint demurrable if, in an action founded upon a contract, it cannot be ascertained from the pleading whether the contract is written, is oral, or is implied by conduct].)
A written contract may either be pleaded word for word or generally "according to its legal intendment and effect." (Construction Protective Services, Inc. v. TIG Specialty Ins. Co. (2002) 29 Cal.4th 189, 198-99.) A written contract can also be pleaded by attaching a copy to the complaint. (See Davies v. Sallie Mae, Inc. (2008) 168 Cal.App.4th 1086, 1091.)

Here, Plaintiff pleads the legal intendment of the contract, as modified, and attaches it to the complaint. (Compl. 6-10 [The parties entered into a contract on October 8, 2018, where Plaintiff was to provide certain services, labor, and materials necessary to complete design and engineering work. Defendant breached the contract by failing to make payments for Plaintiff's services.].) Plaintiff establishes facts to support a breach of contract claim specifically alleging Defendant breached the parties' written contract. (Id. 6.)

The second cause of action for common count is also overruled. Plaintiff is entitled to plead facts or legal theories in the alternative, or even inconsistently. (See Radar Co. v. Stone (1986) 178 Cal.App.3d 10, 29.) "When a common count is used as an alternative way of seeking the same recovery demanded in a specific cause of action, and is based on the same facts, the common count is demurrable if the cause of action is demurrable." (McBride v. Boughton (2004) 123 Cal.App.4th 379, 394.) The breach of contract is adequately pled; therefore, this claim should be overruled. (See Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Zerin (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 445, 460 [The only essential allegations of a common count are "(1) the statement of indebtedness in a certain sum, (2) the consideration, i.e., goods sold, work done, etc., and (3) nonpayment."].

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)
